title: Modelling with quadratic, cubic and power functions
summary: Real data rarely follows a straight line, so this lesson builds the full 2.3 toolkit for curving models: the shape and key features of the quadratic $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ (vertex, axis of symmetry, roots, y-intercept), the cubic $y = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$ and the power function $y = ax^n$; how to fit each to data or a context, almost always through your GDC's regression feature; how to read maxima and minima, intercepts and predictions off the finished model; and how to interpret every parameter back in the language of the problem. Because these are calculator questions, the marks are for choosing the right model, driving the GDC correctly, and writing down the method the examiner needs to see. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks.
learningObjectives: Recognise and describe the key features of quadratic, cubic and power functions: for a quadratic, the vertex, axis of symmetry, roots and y-intercept.
Decide from a scatter plot or a context whether a quadratic, cubic or power model is appropriate, and fit it using GDC regression.
Use a fitted model to find maxima and minima, intercepts and roots, and to make predictions by interpolation or extrapolation.
Interpret the parameters and variables of a model in context, including the meaning of the vertex, the y-intercept and the sign of the leading coefficient.
Present GDC-based working so that method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: From Dots to Curves: Real-World Modelling
simpleExplanation.summary: This topic is about finding the best mathematical curve — a parabola, an S-shape or a power curve — to describe a set of real-world data points, then reading useful facts off that curve: where it peaks, where it crosses the axes, and what it predicts. The GDC does the fitting; your job is to choose the right shape and interpret the result.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you are a tailor and the data points are a customer's measurements. You choose the right pattern (a quadratic, cubic or power function) and adjust it to make a garment (the model) that fits. A good fit lets you predict a size for another item — but predicting far beyond the measurements you took risks an outfit that does not fit at all. That is the difference between interpolation and extrapolation.
steps: Plot and choose. Enter the data into the GDC and make a scatter plot. A single hump or valley (a 'U') suggests a quadratic; an 'S' shape suggests a cubic; a steady curve rising from near the origin suggests a power function.
Fit the model. Run the matching regression (QuadReg, CubicReg or PwrReg). Write the full equation, keeping the parameters to at least 3 significant figures and storing the exact equation in the GDC.
Read the features. Use the GDC's graph tools to find the vertex (maximum or minimum), the x-intercepts (roots) and the y-intercept, and to evaluate predictions.
Interpret in context. Say what each parameter and each feature means for the real situation, attach units, and flag whether a prediction is interpolation (reliable) or extrapolation (question it).

flashcards.back: Its graph is a parabola with exactly one turning point, the vertex. If $a > 0$ the parabola opens upward (vertex is a minimum); if $a < 0$ it opens downward (vertex is a maximum). The constant $c$ is the y-intercept.

The parabola $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ is symmetric about the vertical line $x = -\dfrac{b}{2a}$. The vertex sits on this line, so its x-coordinate is $-\dfrac{b}{2a}$ and its y-coordinate is found by substituting that back in — or read both straight off the GDC's max/min feature.

The roots (or zeros) are the x-values where $y = 0$, i.e. where the parabola crosses the x-axis. A quadratic can have two, one (a repeated root, where the vertex touches the axis) or no real roots. Find them on the GDC with the 'zero' feature.

flashcards.front: Cubic model $y = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$
flashcards.back: A cubic graph is typically an 'S' shape and can have up to two turning points (a local maximum and a local minimum) and up to three real roots. The constant $d$ is the y-intercept.

The shape depends on the exponent $n$. For $n > 0$ the curve passes through the origin and rises; for $n < 0$ it decays like a reciprocal curve and is undefined at $x = 0$. Unlike a polynomial it has a single term, so it usually has no separate y-intercept away from the origin.

The GDC finds the best-fit parameters for a chosen model from a data list: QuadReg for $y = ax^2 + bx + c$, CubicReg for the cubic, PwrReg for $y = ax^n$. You are never asked to compute these by hand — the marks are for entering data correctly and interpreting the output.

A number between 0 and 1 that measures how well the model fits: the closer to 1, the better the fit. If a question does not fix the model, you may fit two and prefer the one with $R^2$ nearer 1. Note $R^2$ compares fit, it does not prove the model is correct beyond the data.

flashcards.front: What the vertex means in context
flashcards.back: The vertex of a quadratic model is the maximum or minimum value of the quantity — the greatest height of a projectile, the least cost of production, the peak of a revenue curve. The x-coordinate says WHEN or at what input it occurs; the y-coordinate says the value there.

The y-intercept is the value of the model when $x = 0$: for a quadratic it is $c$, for a cubic it is $d$. In context this is usually the initial or starting value — an object's launch height, a starting population, a fixed cost.

Interpolation predicts inside the range of the original data and is generally reliable. Extrapolation predicts outside that range and is less reliable — the model may not hold, so its validity should be questioned.

A quadratic model of a real quantity often has two roots, but only one makes sense. Negative times, negative lengths or values outside the modelled range must be rejected with a brief reason — 'time cannot be negative', so take the positive root.

A power model $y = ax^n$ passes through the origin (for $n > 0$) and has no bend-back, so it is monotonic — always rising or always falling. A quadratic has a turning point and a non-zero y-intercept $c$. If data rises then falls, a power model cannot fit it; you need a quadratic.

quickCheck.prompt: For the model $h(t) = -5t^2 + 30t + 10$, the maximum height occurs at:
A ball's height is $h(t) = -4.9t^2 + 20t + 1.5$. Solving $h(t) = 0$ on the GDC gives $t \approx -0.074$ and $t \approx 4.15$. The time the ball hits the ground is:
options: $t \approx 4.15$ s; reject the negative root because time cannot be negative
The mean of the two roots
quickCheck.answer: $t \approx 4.15$ s; reject the negative root because time cannot be negative

quickCheck.prompt: Data rises to a peak and then falls back down. Which model can capture this shape?
options: A power model $y = ax^n$, because it curves
A quadratic $y = ax^2 + bx + c$, because a parabola has a turning point
quickCheck.answer: A quadratic $y = ax^2 + bx + c$, because a parabola has a turning point

quickCheck.prompt: To read the minimum of a fitted quadratic off your GDC you should:
options: Read the smallest y-value in the data list
Graph the stored regression equation and use the 'minimum' feature
quickCheck.answer: Graph the stored regression equation and use the 'minimum' feature

quickCheck.prompt: A power model fitted to data for $2 \le t \le 20$ is used to predict a value at $t = 50$. This prediction is:
options: Extrapolation, so its reliability should be questioned
Exact, because the model is a formula
quickCheck.answer: Extrapolation, so its reliability should be questioned

faq.q: Do I need to memorise how to compute a regression by hand?
faq.a: No. You are never expected to calculate a quadratic, cubic or power regression by hand. You must know how to drive your GDC: enter the data in two lists, choose the matching regression (QuadReg, CubicReg or PwrReg), and interpret the output. The marks are for correct data entry, the right model and sensible interpretation — not arithmetic.

faq.q: What if the question does not tell me which model to use?
faq.a: Start with a scatter plot. A single hump or valley (a 'U') points to a quadratic, an 'S' shape to a cubic, and a steady curve from near the origin to a power model. If two seem plausible you may fit both and compare their coefficients of determination $R^2$, preferring the one nearer 1 — but a context clue (projectile motion, area, cost) often decides it for you.

faq.q: How many significant figures should the parameters have?
faq.a: Give final answers to 3 significant figures unless told otherwise. When you write the model itself, keep more accuracy (4-5 s.f.) or, better, store the exact regression equation in the GDC and evaluate from that. Rounding the parameters and then computing with the rounded values is a classic source of lost accuracy marks.

faq.q: How do I find the vertex, roots or a prediction once I have the model?
faq.a: Store the regression equation as a function (e.g. Y1), graph it, and use the built-in tools: 'maximum' or 'minimum' for the vertex, 'zero' for the roots, and 'value' (or a table) for a prediction at a given input. This avoids re-typing long decimals and keeps full accuracy through to the final answer.

faq.q: Why does the vertex matter so much in modelling?
faq.a: Because it answers the questions examiners ask most: the greatest height, the maximum revenue, the least cost. Its x-coordinate tells you WHEN or at what input the extreme occurs, and its y-coordinate gives the extreme value itself. For $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ it lies on the axis of symmetry $x = -\frac{b}{2a}$, but on Paper 2 you simply read it from the GDC's max/min feature.

sections.content: In the real world, relationships between variables are rarely straight lines — data curves. This lesson takes a set of data points, or a described situation, and fits a mathematical function (a model) that captures the trend, then reads useful facts off it. We focus on three shapes: the quadratic $y = ax^2 + bx + c$, the cubic $y = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$ and the power function $y = ax^n$. Because 2.3 is examined with a calculator, the GDC does the fitting and the feature-finding; the skill being marked is choosing the right model, driving the calculator correctly, and interpreting the result in context. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples below are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice.
The shapes and their key features

Before fitting anything, you need to recognise each shape and name its features, because exam questions ask for those features by name — the vertex, the axis of symmetry, the roots, the intercepts. Start a scatter plot on the GDC and read the shape off the screen.

items: **Quadratic ($y = ax^2 + bx + c$):** a parabola with exactly one turning point, the vertex. It opens upward when $a > 0$ (vertex is a minimum) and downward when $a < 0$ (vertex is a maximum). It is symmetric about the axis of symmetry $x = -\frac{b}{2a}$, meets the y-axis at $(0, c)$, and has up to two roots where it crosses the x-axis.

**Cubic ($y = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$):** typically an 'S' shape with up to two turning points (a local maximum and a local minimum) and up to three roots. It meets the y-axis at $(0, d)$. Good for growth that speeds up, levels off, then changes again.

**Power ($y = ax^n$):** a single-term curve whose shape is set by $n$. For $n > 0$ it rises from the origin with no turning point; for $n < 0$ it decays like a reciprocal curve and is undefined at $x = 0$. It is monotonic — it cannot rise then fall — which is the key clue for when NOT to use it.

sections.content:
For the quadratic $y = ax^2 + bx + c$:
 Axis of symmetry: $x = -\dfrac{b}{2a}$.
 Vertex: the point on that line, x-coordinate $-\dfrac{b}{2a}$, y-coordinate found by substituting it back in.
 y-intercept: $(0, c)$.
 Roots: the solutions of $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, read from the GDC's 'zero' feature.

The sign of the leading coefficient tells the story before you calculate anything. If $a < 0$ a quadratic model has a maximum (a thrown ball, peak revenue); if $a > 0$ it has a minimum (least cost). Stating this early stops you hunting for a maximum on an upward parabola that does not have one.
Fitting a model with your GDC
Once you have chosen a shape, the GDC finds the specific parameters by regression. The process is the same on every calculator: enter, choose, record, store.

items: **Step 1 — Enter.** In the statistics menu, put the independent variable (e.g. time) in one list and the dependent variable (e.g. height) in a second list.
**Step 2 — Choose.** In the regression sub-menu pick the model: QuadReg for quadratic, CubicReg for cubic, PwrReg for power.

**Step 3 — Record.** Run it, checking the correct lists are used. Write the full equation, quoting each parameter to at least 3 significant figures. If shown, note the $R^2$ value as a measure of fit.

**Step 4 — Store.** Save the equation into a function variable (e.g. Y1). Now you can graph it and use max/min, zero and value tools without retyping long decimals — and without introducing rounding error.

sections.content: Worked example 1 — a quadratic model

sections.question:
A small rocket is launched from a platform. Its height, $h$ metres, above the ground is recorded at times $t$ seconds after launch.

| Time, $t$ (s) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 
 |---|---|---|---|---|---| 
 | Height, $h$ (m) | 35 | 50 | 55 | 50 | 35 | 

(a) A quadratic model $h(t) = at^2 + bt + c$ is used. Find $a$, $b$ and $c$. 
(b) Use the model to find the height after 2.5 seconds. 
(c) Determine the maximum height reached according to the model. [6]
sections.solution:
This is a calculator question. Enter the times in one list and the heights in a second, then run quadratic regression.

**(a) Fit the model.** QuadReg on the two lists gives $a = -5$, $b = 30$, $c = 10$, so
$h(t) = -5t^2 + 30t + 10.$  [M1: regression on the GDC]  [A2: all three parameters]

**(b) Prediction at $t = 2.5$.** Because $2.5$ lies between $1$ and $5$ this is interpolation. Evaluate the stored equation:
$h(2.5) = -5(2.5)^2 + 30(2.5) + 10 = -31.25 + 75 + 10 = 53.75.$  [M1: substitute into the model]
The height is $53.8$ m (3 s.f.).  [A1]

**(c) Maximum height.** Since $a = -5 < 0$ the parabola opens downward, so the vertex is a maximum. Graph the stored equation and use the GDC's 'maximum' feature (or note the vertex is at $t = - rac{b}{2a} = - rac{30}{-10} = 3$):
$h(3) = -5(3)^2 + 30(3) + 10 = 55.$  [M1: locate the vertex]
The maximum height is $55$ m, reached at $t = 3$ s.  [A1]

sections.content: When a question says 'find' or 'determine' a model, that is your cue to use GDC regression. Always write the full equation, not just the values of $a$, $b$ and $c$ — the examiner marks the model, and a bare list of numbers can lose a mark.
Worked example 2 — a cubic model

sections.question:
The concentration of a drug in a patient's bloodstream, $C$ (mg/L), is measured $t$ hours after a dose.

| Time, $t$ (h) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 
 |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| 
 | Concentration, $C$ | 0 | 4.8 | 6.4 | 5.4 | 2.8 | 0.5 | 

(a) A cubic model $C(t) = at^3 + bt^2 + ct + d$ is proposed. Use your GDC to find the model. 
(b) Use the model to estimate the maximum concentration and when it occurs. 
(c) According to the model, at what time does the concentration first return to $0$ mg/L after the dose? [6]
sections.solution:
Enter the times in one list and the concentrations in a second, then run cubic regression.

**(a) Fit the model.** CubicReg gives approximately
$C(t) = 0.0648t^3 - 0.870t^2 + 3.19t + 0.0393$  (parameters to 3 s.f.).  [M1: cubic regression]  [A1]

**(b) Maximum concentration.** Graph the stored equation and use the 'maximum' feature over the relevant interval. The local maximum is at about $t = 1.9$ h with $C \approx 6.5$ mg/L.  [M1: use the GDC max feature]  [A1]
So the concentration peaks at roughly $6.5$ mg/L about $1.9$ hours after the dose.

**(c) Return to zero.** Use the GDC's 'zero' feature on the graph after the peak. The relevant root is at about $t = 5.1$ h.  [M1: solve $C(t) = 0$]  [A1]
The model predicts the concentration returns to $0$ mg/L about $5.1$ hours after the dose. (Reject any negative or pre-peak root — only the first zero after the peak is physically meaningful here.)

sections.content: Interpretation and prediction
A model earns its keep only when you interpret it and predict sensibly. Two ideas govern how far you can trust a prediction.

Interpolation: predicting a value INSIDE the range of the original data (e.g. the rocket's height at $t = 2.5$ s, since $2.5$ lies between $1$ and $5$). Generally reliable.\n\nExtrapolation: predicting a value OUTSIDE that range (e.g. the height at $t = 7$ s). Less reliable — the model may not hold beyond the data, so its validity should be questioned.

sections.question:
The value of a vintage car, $V$ in thousands of pounds, is recorded over years $t$ since 2000.

| Years, $t$ | 2 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 
 |---|---|---|---|---|---| 
 | Value, $V$ (£1000s) | 12.5 | 14.2 | 20.1 | 35.8 | 68.3 | 

(a) A power model $V(t) = at^n$ is proposed. Find $a$ and $n$. 
(b) Estimate the car's value in 2018 ($t = 18$). 
(c) Comment on using this model to predict the value in 2050 ($t = 50$). [6]
sections.solution:
The data rises ever more steeply from near the start, with no turning point — a power model is a sensible choice. Enter $t$ in one list and $V$ in a second, then run power regression.

**(a) Fit the model.** PwrReg gives $a \approx 9.76$ and $n \approx 0.822$ (3 s.f.), so
$V(t) = 9.76\,t^{0.822}.$  [M1: power regression]  [A2: both parameters]

**(b) Prediction at $t = 18$.** This is interpolation ($18$ lies between $2$ and $20$). Using the stored equation,
$V(18) = 9.76 	imes 18^{0.822} \approx 109.6.$  [M1: substitute into the model]
The estimated value is about £110 000 (3 s.f.).  [A1]

**(c) Prediction at $t = 50$.** This is extrapolation, far beyond the data (which stops at $t = 20$).  [R1]
The power model predicts unbounded growth, but a real car's value may plateau or fall, so a prediction at $t = 50$ is unreliable and should be treated with caution.  [R1]

sections.content: Interpreting parameters is worth easy marks. For a power model $V = 9.76\,t^{0.822}$, say '$a = 9.76$ is the value (in £1000s) near the start of the record' and '$n = 0.822$ controls how fast the value grows'. For a quadratic, name the vertex as the maximum/minimum and $c$ as the initial value — always with units and always in the words of the context.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing the vertex with a coefficient** — the maximum/minimum of $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ is at $x = -\frac{b}{2a}$ (or read from the GDC), NOT at $x = c$ or at the largest coefficient. The vertex is a point $(x, y)$; quote both coordinates with units.

**Keeping a non-physical root** — a quadratic or cubic model of a real quantity often has two or three roots, but negative times, negative lengths and values outside the modelled window must be rejected with a brief reason ('time cannot be negative'). Take the meaningful root only.

**Forcing a power model onto humped data** — $y = ax^n$ is monotonic and passes through the origin (for $n > 0$); it cannot rise then fall. Data with a peak or trough needs a quadratic or cubic, not a power model.

**Reading a feature off the wrong place** — get the vertex from the GDC's 'maximum'/'minimum' feature and the roots from 'zero'; do not read the largest data value as the maximum or the value of $c$ as the vertex.

**Extrapolating without comment** — predicting outside the data range is allowed, but you must flag it as extrapolation and question its reliability. Treating a far extrapolation as a firm prediction loses the reasoning mark.

**Over-rounding the parameters** — quoting $a$, $b$, $c$ to 3 s.f. and then computing with the rounded values corrupts later answers. Store the exact regression equation and evaluate from it; round only the final answer.
**Writing the parameters but not the model** — 'find the model' means write the full equation $h(t) = -5t^2 + 30t + 10$, not a bare list $a = -5, b = 30, c = 10$.

**Dropping units and context** — a height is '$55$ m', a value is '£110 000'. A bare number left unlabelled, or a parameter never interpreted in context, throws away the interpretation marks these questions are designed to test.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

On the calculator paper the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working — a method mark (M) or an accuracy mark (A) — and an accuracy mark depends on the method mark it follows. Follow-through (FT) means an earlier slip need not cost the marks that depend on it, provided the later step is done correctly on your own figures. The engine also accepts any correctly-rounded value and any equivalent form. But that protection only exists if the method is on the page — a GDC screen described in words earns the method mark, a bare number does not. Study how each mark below is earned.

sections.question: The height of a ball is modelled by $h(t) = -4.9t^2 + 20t + 1.5$ (height in metres, $t$ in seconds). Use your GDC to find the maximum height and the time when the ball hits the ground. [5]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

Store $h(t) = -4.9t^2 + 20t + 1.5$ as a function on the GDC and graph it. Since $a = -4.9 < 0$ the parabola opens downward, so its vertex is a maximum.

**Maximum height.** Use the graph's 'maximum' feature:
the vertex is at $t \approx 2.04$ s, giving $h \approx 21.9$ m.
So the maximum height is $21.9$ m (3 s.f.), reached about $2.04$ s after launch.

**Time it hits the ground.** The ground is $h = 0$. Use the graph's 'zero' feature (or the equation solver):
$-4.9t^2 + 20t + 1.5 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ t \approx -0.074 	ext{ or } t \approx 4.15.$
Time cannot be negative, so reject $t \approx -0.074$ and take the positive root.
The ball hits the ground at $t \approx 4.15$ s (3 s.f.).

---
**How our marking engine awards the 5 marks:**

- **M1 — graph on the GDC.** Awarded for setting up the model on the calculator: storing $h(t)$ and graphing it (or otherwise driving the GDC toward the maximum). This is the method that everything else stands on.
- **M1 — use the maximum feature.** A method mark for using the GDC's 'maximum' (vertex) tool, or equivalently finding $t = - rac{b}{2a}$ and substituting. The engine rewards the approach, so it survives a small slip in the read-off.
- **A1 — 21.9 m.** The accuracy mark for the maximum height $h \approx 21.9$ m (accepting the vertex time $t \approx 2.04$ s). It depends on the M-mark above and is protected by FT: a candidate whose method is right but who reads a slightly different value still scores on their own figure.
- **M1 — solve $h = 0$.** A method mark for setting the height to zero and solving $-4.9t^2 + 20t + 1.5 = 0$ on the GDC (the 'zero' feature or solver). The engine checks that $h = 0$ was solved, not some other equation.
- **A1 — 4.15 s.** The accuracy mark for $t \approx 4.15$ s, with the negative root correctly rejected because time cannot be negative. FT applies — a candidate whose regression or figures differed but who correctly took the positive root of THEIR equation keeps this mark.

**'Accept equivalent forms and correct rounding.'** The engine accepts $21.9$ m written as $21.9$ or $21.94\ldots$ rounded, and accepts $4.15$ s however the positive root is justified in words. Once a correct final value appears, ISW (ignore subsequent working) means later restatements do not lose marks.

**Bottom line:** of the 5 marks, three are method marks that survive a read-off slip, and the accuracy marks are shielded by follow-through. A student who writes only '21.9 m and 4.15 s' with no method risks losing the method marks if a number is off; a student who shows the graph, the max feature, the $h = 0$ solve and the rejection of the negative root keeps the method regardless of a slip in the final digit.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with method marks, dependent accuracy marks and follow-through. So always show the method: store and graph the model, name the feature you used (maximum, minimum or zero), set up the equation you solved, and justify which root you keep. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a number slips — and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

Modelling with curves is the backbone of the applications course. The regression skill here reappears for exponential and trigonometric models, and the vertex, root and intercept features you read off a parabola are the same features you will locate on any function graph. The discipline of choosing a shape from the data, driving the GDC, rejecting non-physical solutions and interpreting parameters in context is exactly what every modelling question on the calculator paper rewards. Master 'choose the shape, fit it, read the feature, say what it means' and the rest of the modelling course becomes variations on a routine you already own.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 modelling question marked: fit the model, find the vertex and roots, and predict with full working
